description: Explains how Requests sessions persist state, prepare requests with session defaults, follow redirects safely, merge cookies/hooks/settings, and update authentication and proxy handling across redirects.
document:
Requests uses sessions to turn a sequence of HTTP calls into a stateful client instead of isolated one-off requests. The user guide describes a session as the object that persists selected parameters, keeps cookies across requests, and benefits from urllib3 connection pooling when several requests go to the same host. In src/requests/sessions.py, `Session` stores the durable defaults that make this work: outgoing headers and query parameters, authentication and proxy preferences, TLS and streaming choices, event hooks, cookies, redirect limits, and mounted transport adapters. The public request methods share the same shape as the top-level Requests API, but they all route through the session so those defaults can be applied consistently.

Session preparation is where per-call input and stored state are combined. `prepare_request` builds the transmissible prepared request after merging request cookies with the session cookie jar, applying environment authentication when trusted, and combining request-level values with session-level defaults. The merge rules are intentionally simple for callers: request values win, missing request values fall back to the session, and dictionary entries set to None at request time remove matching session defaults. The advanced documentation calls out this behavior for session parameters and also warns that method-level cookies are sent only for that call; they are not written back into the session jar. For custom prepared requests, the docs recommend preparing through the session rather than directly from the request object when the caller wants session cookies and other state included.

Hooks follow the same session-versus-request theme, with one important edge case. The session starts with the default response hook list, and hooks added to a session run on every request made by that session. A request can supply its own response hooks, and the preparation logic preserves the expected override behavior instead of accidentally erasing session hooks when an empty hook list appears. The hook tests show that response hooks receive the response plus request arguments, can replace the response by returning a value, and are called in the order supplied. This makes hooks part of session state, but still lets one request choose a different response-handling path.

Sending begins with a prepared request, not a raw request object. The sender fills in default transport settings, resolves proxies when needed, selects an adapter by URL prefix, measures elapsed time, dispatches response hooks, and extracts cookies from the response into the session jar. If redirects are enabled, the response is passed to `resolve_redirects`, which produces the later responses and lets the final response carry the earlier ones in its history. The tests assert that redirect history is a list, does not contain self-references, and preserves each historical response's own request URL instead of overwriting earlier entries.

Redirect handling lives in `SessionRedirectMixin`, which keeps redirect policy separate enough that subclasses can customize how a redirect target is found. The default target reader only follows responses considered redirects and decodes the location header carefully. For each hop, the resolver copies the previous prepared request, consumes or drains the response body so the connection can be released, enforces the session's redirect limit, normalizes scheme-relative and relative locations, preserves fragments when the new location does not supply one, and requotes the URL before sending the next request. Tests cover custom redirect limits, fragment preservation, and custom sessions that override target detection for malformed responses.

Each redirect hop also refreshes request state so credentials and bodies do not leak across the wrong boundary. Browser-compatible method rewriting turns certain redirected POSTs into GETs while leaving HEAD alone, and ordinary redirects drop body-related headers and the body itself; redirects that are meant to preserve the method keep the upload semantics and can rewind a seekable body. Cookies from the redirect response are merged into the next request and then into the session as responses arrive. Authentication is stripped when the host, scheme, or port change in unsafe ways, while a standard same-host HTTP-to-HTTPS upgrade keeps it for compatibility. Proxy settings are recalculated against environment rules, and proxy authorization is rebuilt only where it is appropriate to send.
